introduction: In a multi-user operating system like Linux, security is built into the core design. Without a strict mechanism to control who can read, modify, or execute files, any user or running process could access sensitive configurations, read other users' private data, or corrupt the operating system itself. Linux solves this problem through its file permissions and ownership subsystem.

For DevOps Engineers, managing file system permissions is a fundamental safety barrier. When deploying applications, web server processes (like Nginx) should only have read access to source files, database folders must be restricted to the database user account, and private SSH keys must be locked down to owner-only read access. Understanding how to configure access control lists prevents unauthorized processes from reading or writing configuration secrets.

In this module, you will learn how to read permissions configurations outputs, modify read/write/execute bits using chmod symbolic and numeric notation, change file owners and group owners using chown/chgrp, and configure default creation masks utilizing umask.
what_you_will_learn: The layout of user permissions flags (Read, Write, Execute).
Assigning symbolic and numeric permission modifications using chmod.
Managing file ownership and group memberships utilizing chown and chgrp.
Configuring security restrictions on directory access settings.
Checking default file creation masks via umask.
overview.prerequisites: Completion of Linux Command Line Basics course.
learning_outcomes: Analyze detailed metadata listings of files and directories.
Apply least-privilege permission masks using symbolic and numeric chmod syntax.
Manage system user account permissions using chown and chgrp.

theory.title: Demystifying the rwx Code: Who Can Do What?
theory.definition: A 9-character code that tells Linux exactly who has the right to Read (r), Write (w), or Execute (x) a file or directory.
theory.explanation:
Imagine your computer is a massive shared apartment building. You don't want your roommates reading your private diary or deleting your files, right? Linux is a multi-user system, so it was designed from day one to keep files safe using three groups of access rights:

- User (Owner): The person who created the file.
- Group: A specific collection of users (e.g. the 'DevOps' team or the 'Admins').
- Others: Anyone else who has access to the machine (the general public).

When you run ls -l, you see a code like -rwxr-xr--. Let's break it down:

- The first character tells you what it is: - means a file, and d means a directory (folder).
- The next three rwx are for the User (can Read, Write, and Execute).
- The next three r-x are for the Group (can Read and Execute, but not modify).
- The last three r-- are for Others (can only read the file, completely locked out from changing or running it!).
theory.why_exists: Enforces system security, preventing regular users or compromised web apps from reading confidential configuration files or deleting operating system files.
theory.real_world_example: SSH keys (used to log into remote servers) are so sensitive that Linux will refuse to use them unless you lock down permissions to owner-only read (-r--------).
theory.remember: A directory (folder) needs execute (x) permissions for you to be able to enter it using the cd command! If a folder has read (r) but no execute (x), you can see the file names inside but cannot open any of them.
theory.pro_tip: You can quickly see the user and group owners of all files in the current folder by running ls -l. Look at columns 3 and 4 of the output table.
theory.common_mistakes: Assuming files are safe by default. In a shared server environment, other users can read your files unless you explicitly change their permissions!
theory.best_practices: Always assign the least possible permissions. If a file only needs to be read, don't give it write (w) or execute (x) permissions.

commands.name: chmod (Symbolic)
commands.purpose: Modifies file permissions using letter symbols (u, g, o, r, w, x).
commands.parameters: who: u (user), g (group), o (other), a (all). operator: + (add), - (remove), = (set exact). permission: r, w, x.
commands.output_explanation: Adds write permission for the group users to the file welcome.txt.
commands.when_to_use: Use symbolic mode when you want to quickly add or remove a single specific permission without affecting others.

theory.summary: Linux permissions are divided into User, Group, and Others. The characters r, w, and x represent read, write, and execute permissions respectively.

theory.title: Speaking in Numbers: Numeric chmod Notation
theory.definition: A shorthand math system to set permissions instantly using three numbers (like 755 or 600) representing owner, group, and others.
theory.explanation:
Typing symbolic flags (like u+rwx,g+rx,o+rx) is verbose. Instead, Linux developers use a clever mathematical shorthand where each permission is assigned a number value:

- Read (r) = 4
- Write (w) = 2
- Execute (x) = 1
- No permission (-) = 0

To set permissions, you just add the numbers together for each of the three groups (User, Group, Others):

- 755 (Super common for scripts):
  - User gets 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 (Full control)
  - Group gets 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 (Read & execute only)
  - Others get 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 (Read & execute only)
- 600 (Perfect for secrets/keys):
  - User gets 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 (Read & write)
  - Group gets 0 (Blocked)
  - Others get 0 (Blocked)
- 777 (The nuclear option):
  - Everyone gets 7 (Full access for the whole world!). WARNING: Never use 777 in production, it's a huge security risk!
theory.why_exists: Provides a quick, standard, and automated way to configure file security inside deployment scripts and server setup tools.
theory.real_world_example: Writing chmod 755 deploy.sh to allow your continuous integration runner to execute the script while preventing unauthorized edits.
theory.remember: Think of numeric permission numbers as a sum of weights: 4 (Read), 2 (Write), 1 (Execute). For example, 6 is 4+2 (Read+Write); 5 is 4+1 (Read+Execute). There is only one unique sum for any combination!
theory.pro_tip: Default to 644 for files (owner reads/writes, others read) and 755 for folders/scripts. Use 600 for secret config keys.
theory.common_mistakes: Running chmod 777 on a directory to fix a permission error because it's easy. It's like leaving your front door wide open with a sign saying 'free stuff inside' just because you lost your key!
theory.best_practices: Avoid 777 permissions in production. If an application fails due to permissions, trace the exact user/group account instead of resorting to the nuclear option.

commands.name: chmod (Numeric)
commands.purpose: Sets complete file permissions using numeric octal shorthand values.
commands.parameters: octal_mode: A three-digit number representing owner, group, and others permissions respectively.
commands.output_explanation: Sets script.sh permissions to read/write/execute for owner, and read/execute for group and others.
commands.when_to_use: Use numeric chmod when you need to enforce a complete, exact permission profile on a file or folder.

theory.summary: Numeric notation represents permissions as numbers: 4=read, 2=write, 1=execute. Combine them for user, group, and others (e.g. 755 or 600).

theory.title: Managing Ownership: chown and chgrp
theory.definition: Operations used to change the owner user account or owner group of a file or directory.
theory.explanation:
Every file and folder in Linux belongs to a specific owner user and group. Changing permissions restricts access, but sometimes you need to assign the file to a different owner user or group entirely.

For example, if you copy a configuration file as the administrative user (root), regular applications like web servers (nginx) won't be able to read it. You must change the file owner to the correct account:

- chown: Change Owner (sets the owner user account).
- chgrp: Change Group (sets the owner group).
theory.why_exists: Enables administrators to provision files, assign resources to service accounts, and manage project collaboration groups safely.
theory.real_world_example: Changing a web site's HTML file owner to the nginx web server account (www-data) so the server can display the pages to internet visitors.
theory.remember: Only the system administrator (root or using sudo) can change file ownership to a different user account.
theory.pro_tip: You can change BOTH user and group owner in a single chown command by separating them with a colon: chown user:group filename.
theory.common_mistakes: Changing file ownership to root accidentally, making files uneditable for your local development user accounts.
theory.best_practices: Keep your web and app assets owned by standard service accounts, not the root account, to minimize security attack risks.

commands.name: chown
commands.purpose: Changes the owner user of a file or directory.
commands.parameters: owner_username: The name of the target user account to receive ownership.
commands.output_explanation: Sets welcome.txt owner account to student.
commands.when_to_use: Use this when you copy configuration templates from administrative folders and need to grant standard users control.

commands.name: chgrp
commands.purpose: Changes the owner group of a file or directory.
commands.parameters: group_name: The name of the target user group.
commands.output_explanation: Sets welcome.txt group owner to developers.
commands.when_to_use: Use this to share source code files with specific teams or department groups.

theory.summary: Use chown to change user ownership, and chgrp to change group ownership. You can also do both in one command using chown user:group.

interactive_examples.objective: Change file permissions to read and write for the owner only.
interactive_examples.explanation: Calculates owner as 4+2=6, group as 0, others as 0, restricting access to the owner user account.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting the filename parameter in the command arguments.
interactive_examples.tips: Verify the changed permissions using `ls -l log.txt` immediately after running the command.

interactive_examples.objective: Change the owner user of a file to root.
interactive_examples.explanation: Modifies file metadata settings, setting the root account as the absolute owner.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Attempting chown without appropriate administrative permissions, resulting in permission errors.
interactive_examples.tips: Combine owner and group modifications in one command: `chown root:sudo log.txt`.

lessons.title: 1. Absolute Paths
lessons.definition: An absolute path defines a location starting from the root directory (/).
lessons.explanation: Absolute paths always begin with a forward slash. Example: /home/student/drafts.

lessons.title: 2. Relative Paths
lessons.definition: A relative path defines a location relative to your current working directory.
lessons.explanation: Relative paths do not start with a slash. Double dots (..) represent parent directory.
lessons.instruction: Navigate to the parent directory and then into projects folder.

lessons.title: 3. Permissions Overview
lessons.definition: Linux files have access modes: Read (r), Write (w), and Execute (x).
lessons.explanation: Access modes are split into: Owner (user), Group, and Others (public).
lessons.instruction: Inspect permissions of welcome.txt using detailed ls listing.

lessons.title: 4. Owner Only Access (chmod 600)
lessons.definition: chmod changes permissions settings on a file or directory.
lessons.explanation: Numeric notation: 600 gives read/write to the owner (4+2=6), and zero permissions to others.
lessons.instruction: Restrict log.txt permissions to owner read/write only.
lessons.hint: Create log.txt if needed, then run: chmod 600 log.txt

lessons.title: 5. Executable Scripts (chmod +x)
lessons.definition: +x adds execution permission to the target file.
lessons.explanation: Script files need execute permissions to run like programs in shell.
lessons.instruction: Make the script run.sh executable using symbolic notation.
lessons.hint: Create run.sh first using touch, then type: chmod +x run.sh

lessons.title: 6. Standard Public Exec (chmod 755)
lessons.definition: 755 permits owner read/write/exec, group read/exec, others read/exec.
lessons.explanation: This numeric configuration is standard for public executables and directories.
lessons.instruction: Configure permissions of run.sh explicitly to 755.

lessons.title: 7. Directory Permissions
lessons.definition: Read on directory lists its files, write allows adding/removing files, exec allows entering.
lessons.explanation: To traverse inside folders using cd, you must have the execute flag enabled on them.
lessons.instruction: Inspect directory metadata permissions of drafts using -ld flag.

lessons.title: 8. Recursive Changes (chmod -R)
lessons.definition: The -R flag recursively applies permissions to all subfolders and files.
lessons.explanation: Useful for resetting whole directories permissions at once.
lessons.instruction: Recursively grant 755 permissions to the projects directory tree.

lessons.title: 9. Group Memberships
lessons.definition: Users are mapped to groups to share file permissions collectively.
lessons.explanation: The groups command outputs all group names your user is currently a member of.
lessons.instruction: Print the current active groups of your user.

lessons.title: 10. Check User IDs (id)
lessons.definition: The id command displays user ID (uid), group ID (gid), and group memberships.
lessons.explanation: Used to diagnose user account configurations and identity parameters.
lessons.instruction: Expose full numeric user and group IDs.

lessons.title: 11. Changing Group Owner (chgrp)
lessons.definition: chgrp changes the group ownership of files.
lessons.explanation: Allows group members to share read/write permissions depending on group flags.
lessons.instruction: Change the group ownership of log.txt to sudo.

lessons.title: 12. Changing Owner (chown)
lessons.definition: chown reassigns the owner of a file to a new user account.
lessons.explanation: Typically requires elevated administrative/root permissions to execute.
lessons.instruction: Change owner of log.txt to root.

lessons.title: 13. Creation Mask (umask)
lessons.definition: umask defines the default permission bits set for newly created files.
lessons.explanation: The mask bits are subtracted from system defaults (typically 666 for files, 777 for directories).
lessons.instruction: View your system's current creation mask setting.

lessons.title: 14. Private Folder Traverse
lessons.definition: Removing public read and execute permissions makes a folder private.
lessons.explanation: chmod 700 allows only the owner to browse and write files inside.
lessons.instruction: Restrict projects folder permissions to owner-only read/write/exec (700).

lessons.title: 15. Restrictive Delete
lessons.definition: Deleting files is allowed if the user has write access to the parent folder.
lessons.explanation: Delete the file log.txt using the rm command.

exercises.title: Directory traverse blocks
exercises.problem: Create a directory named `private_workspace` and restrict it using chmod 700. Verify CD traversal fails if group changes.
exercises.objective: Understand how execute (x) flag blocks navigation.

exercises.title: Create write-only script
exercises.problem: Modify an empty script `audit.sh` to allow owner-write only (200).
exercises.objective: Master writing customized security mask configurations.

exercises.title: Symbolic chmod changes
exercises.problem: Add user write and group execute permissions to a script using symbolic syntax.

exercises.title: Audit directory ownership
exercises.problem: Identify the owner and group values of `/etc/passwd`.
exercises.expected_result: root root displayed in metadata column.

exercises.title: Default Creation settings
exercises.problem: Change your active umask shell configuration to a restrictive 0077 and create a file.
exercises.objective: Gain experience configuring active session creation masks.

exercises.title: Group assignments change
exercises.problem: Reassign folder group ownership to system administration groups.

quiz.question: Which command alters read/write/execute permissions?
quiz.explanation: chmod (change mode) sets the permissions mask bits.

incorrect_explanations.umask: umask defines default masks for new creations.

quiz.question: What permissions does chmod 600 grant?
options: Read and write for owner only
quiz.answer: Read and write for owner only
quiz.explanation: 6 maps to 4 (read) + 2 (write) for the owner. 0 removes all access for group and others.

quiz.question: Which command is used to modify the group ownership of a file?
quiz.explanation: chgrp (change group) reassigns file group owners.

incorrect_explanations.groups: groups lists active user group memberships.

quiz.question: In long format listings, what does the first character represent?
quiz.explanation: The first character indicates whether it is a directory (d), regular file (-), or link (l).

incorrect_explanations.Hard link count: Link count is shown as a number after permission characters.
incorrect_explanations.File size: File size is shown after the group name.

quiz.question: How would you symbolically grant execution permission to the group?
quiz.explanation: g refers to group, and +x adds execution rights.

incorrect_explanations.chmod o+x: Adds execution to others/world, not group.
incorrect_explanations.chmod u+x: Adds execution to user/owner, not group.

quiz.question: What value maps to read, write, and execute permissions numerically?
quiz.explanation: Read (4) + Write (2) + Execute (1) sum up to 7.

quiz.question: True or False: A user can enter a directory folder if they have read permissions but not execute permissions.
quiz.explanation: Execute permission (x) is strictly required on folders to traverse inside using cd.

incorrect_explanations.True: Without the execute flag, entering the directory fails with permission denied.

quiz.question: Which flag allows recursive permissions modifications inside a directory tree?
quiz.explanation: The uppercase -R flag tells chmod and chown to recursively modify child files.

incorrect_explanations.-r: Lowercase -r is not the standard recursion flag for chmod.
incorrect_explanations.-f: Force option to ignore error messages.
incorrect_explanations.-v: Verbose logs details of changed file states.

quiz.question: What is the standard permission setting for public directories (like website folders)?
quiz.explanation: 755 allows full owner access, and lets group/others read and navigate inside.

incorrect_explanations.777: Permits public writes, which is a major security risk.
incorrect_explanations.700: Blocks all group and public navigation.

quiz.question: Scenario: A web app script `app.py` is throwing a 'Permission Denied' execution error in your pipeline. It has `-rw-r--r--` permissions. What command fixes this?
quiz.explanation: Adding execution (+x) permission symbolically grants the system execution rights.

incorrect_explanations.chmod 600 app.py: This removes read/exec rights for group/others and blocks execution.
incorrect_explanations.chown root app.py: Changing owner does not grant execution permission bits.
incorrect_explanations.umask 022 app.py: umask is a shell mask setting and cannot be run directly on a file.

resources.summary: This module covers file structures permissions (rwx), octal representation (755, 600), changing permission states with chmod recursively, ownership modification with chown/chgrp, and umask configurations.
resources.cheat_sheet:
chmod [mode] [file] - change permissions
chmod -R [mode] [dir] - recursive permissions
chown [owner] [file] - change owner
chgrp [group] [file] - change group owner
umask [mask] - change defaults creation mask

commands_table.name: chmod
commands_table.description: Alters access permission bits of a file.

revision_notes: User (u) refers to owner, Group (g) to group list, Others (o) to world, All (a) to all.
Octal mappings: r=4, w=2, x=1. Sum them to configure settings.
Folders require execute permissions to be entered.
beginner_mistakes: Using chmod 777 as a generic fix for permission issues, exposing directories to public write access.
Forgetting recursion flag -R when changing website subdirectories.
best_practices: Follow least-privilege principles by restricting configuration secrets to chmod 600.
Ensure web server folders use chmod 755 or 750 for secure directory traversals.

interview_questions.question: How does Linux determine if a file is executable?
interview_questions.answer: By validating if the execution (x) bit is set on the file permissions layout.

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between chmod 755 and 700?
interview_questions.answer: 755 permits public reads and navigations, 700 restricts access exclusively to the owner.

additional_practice: Configure a private assets directory setting chmod 700 on `/home/student/projects`.
Create a read-only configurations file and test writing changes fails.

books.title: Linux Administration Handbook by Evi Nemeth
